meta.title: Shell & Bash
meta.description: Review checklist for shell scripts (Bash, sh, CI/CD pipelines)

items.text: Does the PR have a clear description of what it does and why?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The PR description should explain the purpose of the script change and any relevant context.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Shell scripts are often underdocumented. A clear description is even more important than usual.
baseContent.howToVerify: Read the PR description. Can you understand what changed and why without reading the script?
baseContent.exampleComment: Could you add context about what this deploy script does differently now?
baseContent.keyTakeaway: A good PR description saves reviewer time.

items.text: Does the script have a correct shebang line?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Scripts should start with #!/usr/bin/env bash (for bash) or #!/bin/sh (for POSIX sh). The shebang determines which shell interprets the script.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without a shebang, the script runs in whatever shell the user has, which may not support bash-specific features.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check the first line. #!/usr/bin/env bash is preferred over #!/bin/bash for portability.
baseContent.exampleComment: This script uses bash arrays but has no shebang. Add #!/usr/bin/env bash at the top.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always include a shebang. Use #!/usr/bin/env bash for bash scripts.

items.text: Does the script use set -euo pipefail?

baseContent.whatItMeans: set -e exits on error, -u exits on undefined variables, -o pipefail catches errors in piped commands.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without these, scripts silently continue after errors, which can lead to data corruption, partial deployments, or cascading failures.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for set -euo pipefail near the top of the script, after the shebang.
baseContent.exampleComment: This script is missing set -euo pipefail. Without it, errors are silently ignored.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always use set -euo pipefail. It prevents the most common class of shell script bugs.

baseContent.whatItMeans: Variables should be quoted with double quotes: "$var" not $var. Unquoted variables are subject to word splitting and glob expansion.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unquoted variables with spaces or special characters cause broken commands, accidental file deletion, or security vulnerabilities.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for unquoted $variables, especially in commands like rm, mv, cp. Check for unquoted $@ or $*.
baseContent.exampleComment: rm -rf $DIR is dangerous if DIR contains spaces or is empty. Use rm -rf "$DIR" instead.

baseContent.whatItMeans: eval executes arbitrary strings as shell commands. It is almost never necessary and is a common source of injection vulnerabilities.
baseContent.whyItMatters: eval with user-controlled input enables command injection. Even with trusted input, it makes code hard to reason about.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for eval in the script. Check if the same result can be achieved with arrays, indirect variables, or other constructs.
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses eval to build a command. Use a bash array instead: cmd=("$binary" "$arg1" "$arg2"); "${cmd[@]}".
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Avoid eval. Use arrays for dynamic command construction.

items.text: Are temporary files created safely with mktemp?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Temporary files should be created with mktemp, not hardcoded paths like /tmp/myfile. Use trap to clean up on exit.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Hardcoded temp files are vulnerable to symlink attacks and race conditions in multi-user environments.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for hardcoded /tmp paths. Verify mktemp is used and a trap cleans up the file on EXIT.
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses /tmp/build.log directly. Use tmpfile=$(mktemp) and trap 'rm -f "$tmpfile"' EXIT.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use mktemp for temp files. Always clean up with a trap on EXIT.

items.text: Do error messages go to stderr with a non-zero exit code?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Error messages should be written to stderr (>&2), not stdout. Scripts should exit with non-zero status on failure.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Errors on stdout break piped commands. Non-zero exit codes let callers detect failures.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check error handling for >&2 on error messages and exit 1 on failure.
baseContent.exampleComment: This error message goes to stdout. Use echo 'Error: file not found' >&2 and exit 1.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Errors to stderr, exit non-zero on failure.

items.text: Are [[ ]] conditionals used instead of [ ] in bash scripts?

baseContent.whatItMeans: [[ ]] is a bash builtin that handles quoting, pattern matching, and regex better than the POSIX [ ] command.
baseContent.whyItMatters: [ ] requires careful quoting to avoid errors with empty variables or special characters. [[ ]] handles these automatically.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for [ ] tests in bash scripts. If the script uses #!/bin/bash, prefer [[ ]].
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses [ -z $var ] which breaks if var is unset. Use [[ -z "$var" ]] for safety.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use [[ ]] in bash scripts. Use [ ] only in POSIX sh scripts.

items.text: Does the script pass ShellCheck without warnings?

baseContent.whatItMeans: ShellCheck is a static analysis tool that catches common shell script bugs including quoting issues, incorrect conditionals, and portability problems.
baseContent.whyItMatters: ShellCheck catches bugs that are easy to miss in review, especially quoting and word splitting issues.
baseContent.howToVerify: Run shellcheck on the script. Check CI for ShellCheck integration.
baseContent.exampleComment: ShellCheck SC2086 warns about unquoted variable expansion on line 15. Please quote it.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Run ShellCheck on all shell scripts. Fix warnings, they usually indicate real bugs.

items.text: Is complex logic organized into functions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Scripts longer than ~50 lines should use functions to organize logic, with a main function called at the bottom.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Linear scripts are hard to understand, test, and modify. Functions provide structure and enable reuse.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the script is one long sequence of commands. Could it be organized into named functions?
baseContent.exampleComment: This 200-line script runs top-to-bottom. Consider organizing it into functions like setup(), build(), deploy().
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use functions for structure. Call main at the bottom of the script.

items.text: Is the script safe to run multiple times (idempotent)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Running the script twice should produce the same result. Use mkdir -p, check before creating, and handle existing state.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Scripts often need to be re-run after failures. Non-idempotent scripts can create duplicates, corrupt state, or fail on retry.
baseContent.howToVerify: Think about what happens if the script is interrupted halfway and re-run. Does it handle existing files, databases, or resources?
baseContent.exampleComment: This mkdir will fail if the directory exists. Use mkdir -p instead.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Design scripts to be safely re-runnable. Use -p, if-not-exists, and upsert patterns.

items.text: Are secrets not printed or logged?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Scripts must not echo, print, or log secrets like API keys, tokens, or passwords. Use set +x around secret handling.
baseContent.whyItMatters: CI/CD logs are often accessible to many people. Leaked secrets in logs have caused major security breaches.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for echo of secret variables. Verify set -x (trace mode) is disabled when handling secrets.
baseContent.exampleComment: This echoes the API token for debugging. Remove this line — CI logs are visible to the team.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never log secrets. Use set +x when handling sensitive values.

items.text: Are tool versions pinned in CI scripts?

baseContent.whatItMeans: CI scripts should pin tool and dependency versions rather than using latest or unpinned versions.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unpinned versions cause non-reproducible builds. A new tool release can silently break CI.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for curl | bash installs without version pins. Look for :latest Docker tags.
baseContent.exampleComment: This pulls the latest version of the CLI tool. Pin it to a specific version for reproducibility.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Pin versions in CI. Reproducible builds prevent surprise breakages.

baseContent.whatItMeans: Commands that wait for external resources (network, services, builds) should have timeouts to avoid hanging indefinitely.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A hung CI job wastes compute resources and blocks the pipeline until someone notices.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for commands that could hang (curl, wget, wait loops, service health checks) without timeouts.
baseContent.exampleComment: This curl has no timeout. Add --max-time 30 to prevent hanging if the service is down.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Add timeouts to network calls and wait loops in CI scripts.
